notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - Team / multi-agent setup: a group of agents working together in an application to achieve a common goal.
  - Division of expertise: assign agents specialized roles (plot writer, character writer, ending writer, backend dev, frontend dev, product manager, code executor) so each focuses on a specific subtask.
  - Shared context: teams share the same context so outputs from one agent (e.g., a plot) are visible to others (e.g., character agent).
  - Agent heterogeneity: agents can use different model clients (OpenAI, Claude, Gemini, Olama, OpenRouter, cloud models) and different toolsets or “brains”.
  - Code-executor agent: a dedicated agent that executes code (separate responsibility from coding-generative agents).
  - When to use teams: useful for complex, multi-part tasks; unnecessary overhead for simple tasks where a single LLM may suffice.
  - Team presets in Autogen / agent chat: built-in configurations (e.g., round-robin group chat) for common interaction patterns.
  - Round-robin (round-roin) team: agents take turns in sequence (first → second → third), optionally looping back; this is the most common team configuration.
  - Targeted / single-responder use case: only a specific agent addresses a focused question (like a backend engineer answering a backend doubt) instead of a full round-robin workflow.
  - max_turns (team configuration): the maximum number of agent executions (first execution, second execution, third execution...), not number of full cycles.
  - Participant fields / configuration components: teams include components like agents list, component, description, label, type; participants argument used when constructing group chat teams.
  - Message structure difference: in team-run messages you can observe fields like source and content (whereas single-agent messages may not have source).
  - Run behavior: team.run executes the team workflow and returns final output after completion; run_stream may not show partial output during execution (no streaming visible until finish).
  - Reentrant workflows: teams can send outputs back to earlier agents (looping the flow).

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - Human teams / developer teams: compared agents to human team roles (backend dev, frontend dev, product manager).
  - Jack of all trades vs specialist: “you cannot have someone who is a jack of all trade doing everything properly.”
  - Linear thinking in system design: likened to the linear first→second→third agent execution.

- Any code, commands, or config shown
  - team.run task = task (reference to calling team.run with a task object)
  - max_turns = 3 (example meaning: team will execute up to 3 agent turns)
  - Round-robin group chat / participants argument (used in team construction; "participants" common cause of errors)
  - agent messages include fields: source and content (explicitly noted that team messages include source + content)
  - UI / development steps mentioned: select kernel, open documentation -> agent chat -> pick team preset

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Cost and token usage: longer outputs (e.g., 500 words) increase model usage; prefer shorter prompts for demos (speaker reduced to 50 words).
  - Bias and self-review limitations: a single agent reviewing its own output may propagate biases; using another agent specialized in review/writing can improve results.
  - Shared context necessity: without a framework, passing context between different models requires manual re-sending of previous inputs/outputs (tedious and error-prone).
  - participants argument pitfall: mistaken construction/usage of participants can cause errors when building round-robin group chat teams.
  - Streaming limitation: run_stream or team.run may not show intermediate streaming; output only visible when the whole run completes (can be slow and hard to interrupt).
  - Choose team complexity appropriately: many projects will use single-agent setups; teams are justified mainly for complex multi-part tasks.
  - “max_turns” misunderstanding: it is number of agent executions (turns) not the number of full agent cycles; careful when configuring.

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - Team vs single-agent
    - Team: shared context, specialized agents, better for complex tasks, uses frameworks (Autogen) to manage agent interactions.
    - Single-agent: simpler, may be fine for straightforward tasks, can lack tool support and cross-checking.
  - Interaction types / use cases
    - Round-robin / sequential (first → second → third; can loop back)
    - Targeted single-agent response within a team (only the relevant specialist responds)
  - Example agent roles in story generation
    - Plot agent: create engaging plot, focus on Pokémon's journey
    - Character agent: develop characters, describe Pokémon and villain, motivations/background
    - Ending agent: craft the story ending (uses shared context)
  - Possible model assignments by role
    - Generic tasks → OpenAI (or similar)
    - Coding tasks → cloud models or specialized coding model
    - Execution tasks → code-executor agent (separate)

- Direct quotes worth preserving
  - "a team is group of agent that work together to achieve a common goal."
  - "teams are where any framework and autogen especially shines"
  - "max turns means number of time it agents will kind of executed. First execution, second execution, third execution."
  - "Round robin group chat. A team that runs on a group chart with participant taking turns in roundrobin fashion."
  - "doing this without frameworks are very difficult."
  - "they have the overall shared context."
